hypotheses.claim: The 70.1% hapax ratio is substantially artifactual: stripping word-final glyphs from the set {y,n,l,r,o} reduces unique word types by at least 40%, revealing a core vocabulary consistent with a homophonic substitution cipher on medieval Latin or Italian
evidence_for: Hapax ratio 70.1% is anomalously high relative to natural language corpora of similar size
Word-final glyph set {y,n,l,r,o} accounts for a disproportionate share of word-type diversity
High-confidence belief (0.664) that stripping final glyphs collapses hapax count significantly
Recipes section has largest word count and second-highest entropy â€” consistent with suffix-heavy encoding
evidence_against: If suffix stripping still leaves high hapax ratio, the hypothesis fails
Truly synthetic suffixes would produce more uniform final-glyph distributions than observed
hypotheses.test: Strip all word-final glyphs belonging to {y,n,l,r,o} from every token, recompute vocabulary size and hapax ratio on the resulting stem corpus
hypotheses.test_metric: Post-stripping hapax ratio and unique stem count vs. original 8,261 unique words
hypotheses.test_threshold: Confirmation if post-stripping hapax ratio drops below 40%; denial if it remains above 60%
tests_remaining: Strip all word-final glyphs belonging to {y,n,l,r,o} from every token, recompute vocabulary size and hapax ratio on the resulting stem corpus
hypotheses.reasoning: If a large fraction of apparent vocabulary diversity is produced by mechanical suffix attachment, the underlying cipher table is much smaller than raw statistics suggest, dramatically narrowing the decipherment search space

hypotheses.claim: Currier A and Currier B use two structurally distinct cipher alphabets encoding the same underlying Latin plaintext: the two sub-corpora should exhibit the same bigram entropy and the same Zipf exponent once glyph-level correspondences are remapped
evidence_for: High-confidence belief (0.694) that A and B use distinct cipher tables on the same language
Currier B is more than twice the size of A (23,766 vs 11,022 words), consistent with one scribe being primary
Positional glyph constraints are present in both sub-corpora, suggesting shared underlying grammar
evidence_against: If Zipf exponents diverge significantly between A and B after remapping, the same-language assumption is undermined
Differing entropy values per section could reflect genuine lexical differences between two scribes writing about different topics
hypotheses.test: Compute bigram entropy and Zipf exponent separately for Currier A and Currier B token sets; then apply an optimal glyph-to-glyph mapping (maximize token overlap) and recompute both metrics on the remapped B corpus
hypotheses.test_metric: KL-divergence between A and remapped-B unigram distributions; Zipf exponent difference
hypotheses.test_threshold: Confirmation if KL-divergence < 0.05 and Zipf exponent difference < 0.05 after remapping; denial if KL-divergence > 0.2
tests_remaining: Compute bigram entropy and Zipf exponent separately for Currier A and Currier B token sets; then apply an optimal glyph-to-glyph mapping (maximize token overlap) and recompute both metrics on the remapped B corpus

hypotheses.claim: The zodiac section's anomalously low entropy (3.7149 bits, lowest of all sections) is produced by a label-only encoding where each token is drawn from a small closed vocabulary of fewer than 50 distinct labels, rather than running prose
evidence_for: Zodiac section has only 1,297 words across 12 folios â€” far lower word density than herbal (84 words/folio) or recipes (464 words/folio)
Entropy 3.7149 bits is 0.19 bits below the next-lowest section (astronomical), consistent with a smaller effective alphabet
High-confidence belief (0.243) that zodiac uses homophonic label encoding
Short repeated tokens like 'ol', 'ar', 'or' dominate top-10 word list, plausibly functioning as ordinal or directional labels
evidence_against: If the zodiac vocabulary size is not demonstrably smaller than other sections, the structural explanation loses force
Low entropy could alternatively reflect heavy repetition of a small set of cipher words rather than a label structure
hypotheses.test: Extract the zodiac sub-corpus; compute its unique word count, type-token ratio, and frequency distribution; compare vocabulary coverage curves (what fraction of tokens are covered by the top-N types) against herbal and recipes sections
hypotheses.test_metric: Vocabulary size of zodiac vs. other sections; percentage of zodiac tokens covered by top 20 word types
hypotheses.test_threshold: Confirmation if top 20 word types cover â‰¥ 70% of zodiac tokens and unique zodiac vocabulary â‰¤ 60 types; denial if vocabulary size exceeds 150 or top-20 coverage is below 50%
tests_remaining: Extract the zodiac sub-corpus; compute its unique word count, type-token ratio, and frequency distribution; compare vocabulary coverage curves (what fraction of tokens are covered by the top-N types) against herbal and recipes sections
hypotheses.reasoning: Identifying the zodiac as a closed-label section would isolate it as a potential Rosetta-style entry point where glyph-to-label mappings could be tested against known zodiac names

hypotheses.claim: The strong positional constraints on word-initial glyphs {o,c,q,s,d} and word-final glyphs {y,n,l,r,o} are artifacts of a structured nulls-and-affixes system rather than natural phonotactics: removing these positional glyphs as null markers should produce a residual corpus whose glyph entropy approaches Latin's ~4.0 bits
evidence_for: Glyph entropy 3.8627 bits is measurably below Latin (~4.0) and Italian (~3.95), consistent with entropy suppression from null/affix glyphs
Positional constraints are among the highest-confidence beliefs in the system (0.624, 0.608, 0.605)
Zipf exponent 0.8946 is sub-Zipfian, which can result from systematic morphological prefixing/suffixing that splits natural-language word types
evidence_against: Natural Semitic or agglutinative languages also exhibit strong positional constraints without nulls
If residual entropy after stripping still falls below 3.9 bits, a pure nulls explanation is insufficient
hypotheses.test: Remove all word-initial tokens from {o,c,q,s,d} and all word-final tokens from {y,n,l,r,o} at the glyph level; recompute glyph-level Shannon entropy and Zipf exponent on the residual glyph sequence
hypotheses.test_metric: Residual glyph entropy (bits) and Zipf exponent R-squared after stripping positional glyphs
hypotheses.test_threshold: Confirmation if residual glyph entropy rises to â‰¥ 3.95 bits (within 0.05 of Italian); denial if residual entropy remains â‰¤ 3.87 bits or R-squared drops below 0.85
tests_remaining: Remove all word-initial tokens from {o,c,q,s,d} and all word-final tokens from {y,n,l,r,o} at the glyph level; recompute glyph-level Shannon entropy and Zipf exponent on the residual glyph sequence

hypotheses.claim: The text-only section (entropy 3.9016 bits, 7 folios) represents unenciphered or minimally enciphered running text in medieval Latin or Northern Italian, and its word-frequency distribution should match a Zipf exponent significantly closer to 1.0 than the manuscript-wide exponent of 0.8946
evidence_for: Text-only section has the highest entropy of all sections (3.9016 bits), closest to natural Latin/Italian (~4.0/~3.95)
High-confidence belief (0.677) that text-only section approximates natural language most closely
7 folios is a small but statistically tractable sub-corpus for frequency analysis
Absence of illustrations in text-only folios removes the label/caption effect that depresses entropy elsewhere
evidence_against: Small sample (2,349 words) limits statistical power for reliable Zipf fitting
Elevated entropy could reflect a different cipher mode rather than absence of cipher
hypotheses.test: Compute Zipf exponent and R-squared for the text-only section in isolation; compute KL-divergence between text-only unigram distribution and a reference medieval Latin corpus unigram distribution
hypotheses.test_metric: Text-only Zipf exponent; KL-divergence against Latin reference
hypotheses.test_threshold: Confirmation if text-only Zipf exponent â‰¥ 0.95 and KL-divergence < 0.15; denial if Zipf exponent < 0.85 or KL-divergence > 0.30
tests_remaining: Compute Zipf exponent and R-squared for the text-only section in isolation; compute KL-divergence between text-only unigram distribution and a reference medieval Latin corpus unigram distribution
hypotheses.reasoning: If the text-only section statistically resembles natural Latin more than any cipher section, it becomes the highest-priority target for direct frequency-matching decipherment attempts
